chunk_summary: Vitamin A supplementation in SAM focuses on eye findings, measles risk, and avoiding duplication with fortified foods. Dosages are age-specific, and corneal involvement requires urgent care.
learning_objectives: Understand the indications for vitamin A supplementation in severe acute malnutrition (SAM).
Learn age-specific dosing guidelines for vitamin A in SAM management.
Recognize the importance of assessing eye signs and measles risk in SAM patients.
Differentiate vitamin A therapy from measles vaccination in clinical practice.
key_facts: Vitamin A dosages: 50,000 IU (<6 months), 100,000 IU (6–12 months), 200,000 IU (>12 months).
High-dose vitamin A is given on day 1 for eye signs of deficiency or suspected measles.
Repeat dosing is avoided if a high dose was given in the preceding month unless indicated.
Corneal involvement in SAM is an emergency requiring urgent ophthalmic care and vitamin A therapy.
Vitamin A therapy prevents complications, while measles vaccination is a preventive measure post-stabilization.
reference_quote: Treat any corneal involvement as an emergency requiring urgent ophthalmic care and vitamin A therapy. (Source: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK154454/)
document_key_topics: Management of eye signs and measles risk
Distinction between vitamin A therapy and measles vaccination
